content:
4 min read
1 hour ago

--

There are ideas that stay with you for years.
Not because they’re bad ideas, but because you know exactly how much work they will take before you can even answer the most important question:
“Does this actually have value?”
For me, one of those ideas was the Jails Inspector.
A print screen of Jails Inspector in Alpha versionFor nearly eight years I imagined having something similar to the browser DevTools available for Jails, a visual way to inspect an application’s component tree, understand how components relate to each other, inspect state, and make debugging significantly easier.
The problem wasn’t the idea.
It was everything that came before proving the idea.

The Cost of Validation
As developers, we often romanticize building.
But experienced developers know that the most expensive part of a project isn’t necessarily writing code.
It’s investing dozens or hundreds of hours into something that might not even be useful.
Every time I thought about building the Inspector, my mind immediately expanded the scope:

• creating the Chrome extension

• communication between the page and the extension

• collecting component metadata

• rendering an interactive graph

• selecting components

• synchronizing state

• handling edge cases

• polishing the UI
By the time I mentally finished planning it…
…I had already convinced myself it wasn’t worth starting.
Not because it wasn’t valuable.
Because validating that value was simply too expensive.

AI Changed the Economics of Side Projects
This wasn’t a case of AI making me “10x more productive.”
It changed something much more fundamental.
It reduced the cost of experimentation.
Instead of asking:
“Is this worth spending several weekends building?”
I could ask:
“Can I validate this idea this afternoon?”
That’s a completely different equation.

Three Hours Later…
I opened my editor.
Started describing what I wanted.
Component tree.
Interactive nodes.
Component metadata.
State visualization.
Selection.
Communication with the extension.
Instead of writing implementation after implementation, I spent my time reviewing, refining, testing, and asking for improvements.
Around three hours later, I had something that actually worked.
Not a mockup.
Not static screens.
A real Chrome extension.
A real MVP.
And, most importantly…
I finally had an answer to the question I had postponed for years.
Yes. This idea has value.

The Surprising Part
What surprised me wasn’t how quickly the MVP was built.
It was how easy it became to continue improving it.
Normally, after an MVP comes the tedious part:

• fixing bugs

• adjusting layouts

• polishing interactions

• refactoring

• adding small features
With AI, each improvement became a conversation instead of a development session.
I wasn’t fighting boilerplate.
I wasn’t context-switching constantly.
I wasn’t spending hours searching Stack Overflow for small implementation details.
I was simply evolving the product.
In fact, almost every improvement so far has happened without me writing code directly.
I describe the behavior.
Review the result.
Adjust what I want.
Repeat.

The Result
Today the Inspector allows developers to visualize their application’s component hierarchy as an interactive graph.
The selected component immediately exposes useful information like:

• component hierarchy

• parent/child relationships

• component data

• applied CSS classes

• internal state
It turns something that previously required mental mapping into something you can simply look at.
The screenshot below is still an early version, but even this MVP has already changed how I debug Jails applications.

AI Didn’t Replace the Idea
This is the part I find most interesting.
The idea wasn’t generated by AI.
The architecture wasn’t discovered by AI.
The product vision wasn’t invented by AI.
The problem had been sitting in my notebook for nearly eight years.
What AI removed was the enormous execution barrier between:
“I think this would be useful.”
and
“Now I know it is.”
That’s a profound difference.

How Many Ideas Are Waiting?
I suspect many developers have their own version of this story.
An extension.
A library.
A debugging tool.
A side project.
Something they’ve wanted to build for years but couldn’t justify the investment before validating it.
AI changes that calculation.
Not because every generated line of code is perfect.
But because it dramatically reduces the cost of answering one simple question:
“Is this worth building?”
Sometimes, that’s all an idea needs to finally come to life.
If there’s one thing this project taught me, it’s that AI isn’t just accelerating software development.
It’s allowing ideas that would have remained forever in the “maybe someday” folder to finally become real.

A Sneak Peek of What’s Coming
Although the Inspector is already proving useful, it’s still in alpha.
I’m using it daily while developing Jails itself, and every debugging session ends up revealing another feature I wish I had years ago.
My goal is to make it publicly available over the next few weeks.
The current component tree visualization is only the beginning. Some of the features I’m actively working on include:

• State update tracking — making it easy to see exactly which components were affected by a state change.

• State mutation tracing — showing where state updates originated, making it much easier to understand the flow of data through an application.

• Frame rate monitoring — helping identify rendering slowdowns and performance bottlenecks while interacting with the application.
None of these ideas came from trying to compete with existing DevTools.
They came from years of building applications and repeatedly asking myself the same questions during debugging:

• Why did this component update?

• What triggered this state change?

• Which components were actually affected?

• Is this interaction causing rendering performance issues?
These are the kinds of answers I’ve always wanted while developing with Jails, but never had available.
Now, for the first time, they’re becoming real.
